headline: The Sabah Claim That Won't Die — What the Philippines Wants, What Malaysia Won, and What Nobody Settled
context: The Philippines reaffirmed its sovereignty claim over Sabah in February 2026 after a NAMRIA map depicted the state as Philippine territory. Malaysia has won every international court case — Paris (2025), The Hague (2023), Netherlands (2024) — but all on procedure. No court has ruled on the underlying 1878 'ceded vs leased' question because Malaysia has never consented to ICJ jurisdiction.
revisions_made: CORRECTED (Stage 3): February 2026 map was renewed attention to a 2012 NAMRIA cartograph, not a newly published map. Softened to 'depicted Sabah' not 'newly published.'
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Lahad Datu casualties revised — 78 total casualties including 68 militants killed and 10 Malaysian security personnel, not '56 militants, 6 civilians, 6 security.'

CORRECTED (Stage 3): Cobbold Commission finding nuanced — one-third strong support, one-third conditional, remaining third split between independence-first and rejection. Not simply 'two-thirds supported.'
CORRECTED (Stage 3): RA5446 Section 2 does not draw Sabah into baselines — it says baselines are 'without prejudice' to the Sabah claim. More precise than 'includes Sabah in baselines.'
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Sabah special grant has been reviewed and increased — RM125.6M for 2022. Not frozen at RM26.7M. Removed this claim entirely.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Sultan's name more commonly rendered as Jamalul Alam, not Jamal ul-Azam.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Annual payments continued until 2012, terminated 2013 after Lahad Datu.
ADDED (Stage 4): 810,443 foreigners in Sabah per Census 2020 (23.7% of state population).
ADDED (Stage 4): Sabah GDP RM84.3B (2024), exports RM61.3B led by crude petroleum, palm oil, LNG.
ADDED (Stage 4): Philippine CHR advisory on statelessness risk for people of Filipino descent in Sabah.
ADDED (Stage 4): UNICEF case study on undocumented families, birth registration barriers, healthcare access.
ADDED (Stage 4): Bajau Laut evictions in Semporna 2024 (ISEAS documented).
ADDED (Stage 4): Counter-argument — Cobbold was consultative not referendum; the 'self-determination' claim is strong but not unassailable.
ADDED (Stage 4): Counter-argument — Malaysia's RM5,300 annual payments can be read as acknowledging continuing obligation.
ADDED (Stage 4): International parallels — Gibraltar, Falklands, Kashmir, Hong Kong.
STRENGTHENED COURAGE (Stage 5): Named Project IC as real demographic engineering.
STRENGTHENED COURAGE (Stage 5): Named Philippines' claim as domestic political survival, not genuine sovereignty pursuit.
STRENGTHENED COURAGE (Stage 5): Named the class reality — elites play sovereignty chess, poor border communities pay.
STRENGTHENED COURAGE (Stage 5): Acknowledged Malaysia's 50-year payments were political pragmatism, not sovereignty concession.
ADDRESSED (Stage 2): Sulu-origin communities treated as subjects with agency, not just security objects.
ADDRESSED (Stage 2): Economic dimension added — Sabah's RM84.3B GDP and resource revenues.

NOT ADOPTED (Stage 5): Framing ceded-vs-leased as 'legally irrelevant' — T4A presents both sides honestly. The question IS unresolved by any court of competent jurisdiction, even if effective control and self-determination make Malaysia's position overwhelmingly stronger.
